title: Information Security Awareness
description: A foundational module covering the essential principles of information security, common threats facing organisations, and the practical steps every employee must take to protect sensitive information.

sections.title: Why Information Security Matters
sections.content:
Information is one of your organisation's most valuable assets. Whether it is customer data, financial records, intellectual property, or internal communications, the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of this information underpins business operations, regulatory compliance, and stakeholder trust.

A single security breach can result in significant financial losses, reputational damage, regulatory penalties, and loss of customer confidence. High-profile incidents regularly demonstrate that organisations of all sizes and sectors are targets. The cost of prevention is invariably lower than the cost of remediation.

ISO 27001 provides a systematic framework for managing information security risks. It requires organisations to establish, implement, maintain, and continually improve an Information Security Management System (ISMS). Every person who handles organisational information plays a role in making this system effective.

Your awareness and behaviour are the first line of defence. Technical controls alone cannot prevent all security incidents — human decisions and actions are critical to maintaining a secure environment.

sections.title: Common Threats to Information Security
sections.content:
Organisations face a wide range of threats to their information assets. These include external attacks such as malware, ransomware, and distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, as well as internal threats from negligent or malicious insiders. Understanding the threat landscape helps you recognise risks before they materialise.

Malware encompasses viruses, trojans, worms, and spyware that can infiltrate systems through email attachments, malicious websites, or infected removable media. Ransomware is a particularly damaging variant that encrypts your files and demands payment for their release. These attacks frequently target employees through deceptive emails or downloads.

Phishing remains one of the most prevalent attack vectors. Attackers craft convincing emails, messages, or websites designed to trick you into revealing credentials, clicking malicious links, or transferring funds. Spear-phishing targets specific individuals using personal information gathered from public sources, making these attacks harder to detect.

Other common threats include man-in-the-middle attacks on unsecured networks, brute-force attacks against weak passwords, and supply chain compromises where attackers target a trusted third party to gain access to your organisation. Staying informed about current threats is essential to recognising and avoiding them.

sections.title: Social Engineering Awareness
sections.content:
Social engineering exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities. Attackers manipulate people into divulging confidential information, granting access, or performing actions that compromise security. These attacks succeed because they exploit trust, urgency, authority, and helpfulness.

Common social engineering techniques include pretexting (creating a fabricated scenario to obtain information), baiting (leaving infected USB drives or offering enticing downloads), tailgating (following an authorised person into a secure area), and vishing (voice phishing over the telephone). Attackers often impersonate IT support, senior management, suppliers, or auditors to lend credibility to their requests.

To defend against social engineering, always verify the identity of anyone requesting sensitive information or access, regardless of their claimed role or authority. Be suspicious of unsolicited contact that creates a sense of urgency or pressure. Never share passwords, access codes, or confidential data over the phone or email without following established verification procedures.

If something feels unusual or too good to be true, trust your instincts and report it. Legitimate requests will withstand verification. Your organisation's security team would always prefer to investigate a false alarm than deal with a successful attack.

sections.title: Password Hygiene and Authentication
sections.content:
Passwords remain a primary means of authentication, and weak or compromised passwords are a leading cause of security breaches. Good password hygiene is one of the simplest and most effective measures you can take to protect organisational information.

Create strong, unique passwords for each account and system. A strong password is at least 12 characters long and combines upper and lower case letters, numbers, and special characters. Alternatively, use a passphrase — a sequence of random words that is easy to remember but difficult to guess. Never reuse passwords across different systems, as a breach on one platform can then compromise all your accounts.

Use a password manager approved by your organisation to generate and store complex passwords securely. Enable multi-factor authentication (MFA) wherever it is available. MFA requires a second form of verification — such as a code from an authenticator app or a hardware token — making it significantly harder for attackers to gain access even if they obtain your password.

Never share your passwords with anyone, including colleagues or IT support. Legitimate IT staff will never ask for your password. If you suspect a password has been compromised, change it immediately and report the incident to your security team.

sections.title: Physical Security Considerations
sections.content:
Information security extends beyond digital systems. Physical security measures protect the hardware, documents, and environments where information is stored and processed. A breach of physical security can be just as damaging as a cyber attack.

Secure your workstation whenever you leave it unattended, even briefly. Lock your screen using the keyboard shortcut (Ctrl+L on Windows, Command+Control+Q on macOS) and ensure sensitive documents are not left visible on your desk. Follow your organisation's clear desk and clear screen policy to reduce the risk of information being observed or taken by unauthorised individuals.

Be vigilant about physical access controls. Do not hold doors open for unknown individuals or allow tailgating into secure areas. Wear your identification badge visibly and challenge or report anyone without proper identification. Visitors should be escorted and supervised in accordance with your organisation's visitor management procedures.

Dispose of sensitive information securely. Paper documents containing confidential data must be shredded using cross-cut shredders. Electronic storage media such as hard drives, USB devices, and optical discs must be sanitised or destroyed in accordance with your organisation's data disposal procedures before being discarded or repurposed.

sections.title: Reporting Security Incidents
sections.content:
Prompt reporting of security incidents and suspected vulnerabilities is essential to minimising damage and preventing recurrence. Every employee has a responsibility to report anything that could indicate a security breach, regardless of how minor it may seem.

A reportable event includes, but is not limited to: suspicious emails or messages, unexpected system behaviour, loss or theft of devices or documents, unauthorised access to systems or areas, accidental disclosure of sensitive information, and any suspected policy violations. When in doubt, report it — it is always better to raise a concern that turns out to be benign than to ignore a genuine threat.

Report incidents through your organisation's designated channels as quickly as possible. This typically involves contacting your IT security team, using an incident reporting tool, or notifying your line manager. Provide as much detail as you can, including what happened, when it occurred, what systems or data may be affected, and any actions you have already taken.

Do not attempt to investigate or remediate the incident yourself unless you have been trained and authorised to do so. Well-intentioned actions can inadvertently destroy evidence or worsen the situation. Your organisation's incident response team has the expertise and tools to handle incidents effectively. There should be no blame or negative consequences for reporting in good faith.

title: Access Control & Identity Management
description: This module covers the principles and practices of access control within an ISO 27001 framework, including identity management, authentication, authorisation, and the ongoing review of access rights.

sections.title: The Principle of Least Privilege
sections.content:
The principle of least privilege states that users, systems, and processes should be granted only the minimum level of access required to perform their legitimate functions. This foundational concept limits the potential damage from accidents, errors, and malicious activity by reducing the attack surface.

When access rights exceed what is needed, the risk of unauthorised data exposure, accidental modification, or deliberate misuse increases significantly. An employee with broad access who falls victim to a phishing attack gives the attacker the same elevated permissions. Restricting access to what is genuinely necessary contains the blast radius of any single compromise.

Implementing least privilege requires a clear understanding of each role's responsibilities and the information assets they need to access. Access should be granted based on job function, not seniority or convenience. Default configurations should deny access unless explicitly granted, and temporary access for specific tasks should be time-limited and revoked promptly.

This principle applies equally to human users, service accounts, applications, and automated processes. Organisations should regularly challenge whether existing access levels remain appropriate, particularly when roles change, projects conclude, or systems are decommissioned.

sections.title: Access Rights Management
sections.content:
Effective access rights management encompasses the entire lifecycle of user access: from initial provisioning through modifications to eventual de-provisioning. A formal process ensures that access is consistently granted, documented, and controlled.

Access provisioning should be triggered by an approved request, typically initiated by a line manager and authorised by the information asset owner. The request should specify the systems, data, and level of access required, along with the business justification. Automated provisioning through identity management systems reduces delays and errors while maintaining an audit trail.

When an employee changes role, transfers to a different department, or takes on additional responsibilities, their access rights must be reviewed and adjusted accordingly. Previous access that is no longer relevant should be revoked — accumulation of access rights over time (known as privilege creep) is a common and serious risk.

De-provisioning is critical when employees leave the organisation, whether through resignation, retirement, or termination. Access to all systems and facilities must be revoked promptly, ideally on or before the departure date. Orphaned accounts — active credentials belonging to former employees — are a significant vulnerability that attackers actively seek to exploit.

sections.title: Privileged Access Management
sections.content:
Privileged accounts — such as system administrators, database administrators, and root or superuser accounts — have elevated permissions that can bypass normal security controls. The compromise or misuse of a privileged account can have catastrophic consequences, making their management a top priority.

Privileged access should be tightly controlled and separately managed from standard user access. Individuals who require privileged access should use separate accounts for administrative tasks and standard accounts for day-to-day activities such as email and web browsing. This separation reduces the risk of privileged credentials being exposed through routine activities.

Organisations should implement privileged access management (PAM) solutions that provide secure vaulting of privileged credentials, session recording, just-in-time access provisioning, and automated credential rotation. These tools ensure that privileged access is auditable, time-limited, and revocable.

All privileged access activity should be logged and monitored. Unusual patterns — such as access outside normal working hours, bulk data extraction, or configuration changes — should trigger alerts for investigation. Regular reviews of privileged accounts must verify that each account remains necessary, that its scope is appropriate, and that the assigned individuals still require this level of access.

sections.title: Authentication Best Practices
sections.content:
Authentication is the process of verifying that a user, device, or system is who or what it claims to be. Strong authentication mechanisms are essential to preventing unauthorised access and form the gateway to all access control decisions.

Multi-factor authentication (MFA) should be enforced for all remote access, privileged accounts, and systems processing sensitive information. MFA combines two or more independent factors: something you know (password or PIN), something you have (hardware token, smart card, or authenticator app), and something you are (biometric verification). SMS-based one-time codes are better than single-factor authentication but are vulnerable to SIM-swapping attacks; authenticator apps or hardware tokens are preferred.

Password policies should mandate sufficient length and complexity while avoiding requirements that lead to insecure behaviour, such as frequent mandatory changes that encourage users to write passwords down or make minimal incremental changes. Modern guidance from bodies such as the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) recommends allowing longer passphrases, blocking commonly breached passwords, and relying on MFA rather than frequent rotation.

Single sign-on (SSO) solutions can improve both security and user experience by reducing the number of credentials users must manage while centralising authentication controls. Federated identity management enables secure authentication across organisational boundaries. Regardless of the mechanism, all authentication events should be logged to support monitoring and incident investigation.

sections.title: Access Reviews and Recertification
sections.content:
Access reviews are a periodic verification that existing access rights remain appropriate, necessary, and aligned with current business requirements. Without regular reviews, access controls degrade over time as roles change, projects end, and organisational structures evolve.

Access reviews should be conducted at defined intervals — typically quarterly for privileged and high-risk access, and at least annually for standard access. Reviews should be performed by the relevant information asset owners or line managers, who are best placed to determine whether access remains justified for each individual.

The review process should examine user accounts, group memberships, role assignments, and any temporary or emergency access that may not have been revoked. Particular attention should be given to accounts with no recent activity, users who have changed roles, shared or generic accounts, and any access that was granted on an exceptional basis.

Findings from access reviews must be acted upon promptly. Unnecessary or excessive access should be revoked, and any anomalies investigated. The results of each review, including decisions made and actions taken, should be documented to demonstrate compliance and support continuous improvement. Automated tools can significantly reduce the administrative burden of access reviews while improving accuracy and consistency.

title: Information Security Risk Assessment
description: This module provides a practical understanding of information security risk assessment as required by ISO 27001, covering methodology, asset identification, threat analysis, risk evaluation, and treatment options.

sections.title: Risk Assessment Methodology
sections.content:
ISO 27001 requires organisations to define and apply a systematic information security risk assessment process. The methodology must be documented, repeatable, and capable of producing consistent and comparable results. It forms the foundation of the ISMS and drives decisions about which security controls to implement.

A risk assessment methodology typically includes the following stages: establishing the context, identifying risks, analysing risks, evaluating risks, and determining risk treatment. The organisation must define its risk criteria — including how likelihood and impact are measured, how risk levels are determined, and what level of risk is acceptable.

There are several recognised approaches to risk assessment. Qualitative methods use descriptive scales (such as low, medium, high) and are useful for initial assessments or where quantitative data is limited. Quantitative methods assign numerical values to likelihood and impact, enabling more precise comparison and prioritisation. Many organisations use a hybrid approach that combines both.

The chosen methodology should be proportionate to the organisation's size, complexity, and risk appetite. It must be understood by those conducting assessments and supported by appropriate tools and templates. Consistency is essential — the same methodology should be applied across all areas of the organisation to allow meaningful comparison and aggregation of risks.

sections.title: Asset Identification and Valuation
sections.content:
Effective risk assessment begins with identifying the information assets that need protection. An information asset is anything that has value to the organisation and could be compromised. This includes data (customer records, financial information, intellectual property), systems (servers, applications, databases), physical assets (laptops, mobile devices, storage media), and people (knowledge, skills, expertise).

Asset identification should be systematic and comprehensive. Work through business processes to identify the information assets that support them, who owns them, where they are stored, how they are processed, and who has access. Maintain an asset register that records each asset, its owner, its classification, and its location.

Asset valuation determines the relative importance of each asset to the organisation. This is not necessarily a financial figure — it reflects the impact that would result from a loss of confidentiality, integrity, or availability. Consider regulatory obligations, contractual requirements, business continuity implications, and reputational consequences when assessing value.

Asset owners are responsible for ensuring their assets are appropriately classified, protected, and included in risk assessments. Ownership should be clearly assigned and documented. Without a clear understanding of what you are protecting and why it matters, risk assessment becomes an abstract exercise rather than a practical tool for decision-making.

sections.title: Threat and Vulnerability Analysis
sections.content:
Once assets have been identified, the next step is to determine what could go wrong. Threat analysis identifies the potential sources of harm to information assets, while vulnerability analysis identifies the weaknesses that could be exploited by those threats.

Threats can be categorised as deliberate (cyber attacks, espionage, sabotage, theft), accidental (human error, misconfiguration, accidental deletion), or environmental (fire, flood, power failure, hardware failure). Threat intelligence — from industry bodies, government agencies, and commercial feeds — helps organisations understand which threats are most relevant to their sector and geography.

Vulnerabilities are weaknesses in systems, processes, or controls that could be exploited by a threat. These include technical vulnerabilities (unpatched software, weak encryption, insecure configurations), procedural vulnerabilities (lack of formal processes, inadequate training), and physical vulnerabilities (insufficient access controls, poor environmental protection). Regular vulnerability scanning, penetration testing, and process reviews help identify these weaknesses.

The combination of a threat exploiting a vulnerability results in a risk scenario. For each identified asset, consider which threats are relevant, which vulnerabilities exist, and what the realistic scenarios for compromise look like. This analysis provides the basis for assessing the likelihood and impact of each risk.

sections.title: Risk Evaluation Criteria
sections.content:
Risk evaluation involves determining the level of each identified risk and deciding whether it is acceptable or requires treatment. This requires predefined criteria for assessing likelihood, impact, and overall risk level, as well as a clear definition of the organisation's risk appetite.

Likelihood reflects how probable it is that a threat will exploit a vulnerability. Factors to consider include the motivation and capability of threat actors, the exposure of the asset, the effectiveness of existing controls, and historical incident data. Use a consistent scale — for example, rare, unlikely, possible, likely, and almost certain — with clear definitions for each level.

Impact reflects the consequences if the risk materialises. Consider financial loss, operational disruption, legal and regulatory penalties, reputational damage, and harm to individuals. Impact should be assessed across the three dimensions of information security: confidentiality (unauthorised disclosure), integrity (unauthorised modification), and availability (loss of access). Use a consistent scale with defined thresholds.

The combination of likelihood and impact produces an overall risk level, typically represented in a risk matrix. The organisation's risk appetite defines the threshold above which risks must be treated. Risks below this threshold may be accepted, but this acceptance must be a conscious, documented decision by an appropriate authority. Risk evaluation should consider the cumulative effect of multiple lower-level risks, which together may represent an unacceptable exposure.

sections.title: Risk Treatment Options
sections.content:
Once risks have been evaluated, the organisation must decide how to address those that exceed the acceptable threshold. ISO 27001 recognises four risk treatment options: modify (mitigate), share (transfer), avoid, and retain (accept).

Risk modification involves implementing controls to reduce the likelihood or impact of a risk to an acceptable level. This is the most common treatment option. Controls may be technical (firewalls, encryption, access controls), organisational (policies, procedures, training), physical (locks, CCTV, environmental controls), or legal (contracts, non-disclosure agreements). ISO 27001 Annex A provides a comprehensive catalogue of controls that organisations should consider.

Risk sharing transfers the impact of a risk to a third party, typically through insurance or by outsourcing the activity to a specialist provider. It is important to note that accountability for information security cannot be transferred — the organisation remains responsible for ensuring adequate protection. Risk avoidance eliminates the risk entirely by ceasing the activity or changing the business process that creates the exposure.

Risk retention means accepting the risk without further treatment, which is appropriate when the risk level is within the organisation's appetite or when the cost of treatment outweighs the potential impact. All risk treatment decisions must be documented in a risk treatment plan, approved by the relevant risk owners, and tracked to completion. Residual risk — the risk remaining after treatment — must be formally accepted by management and monitored on an ongoing basis.

title: Security Incident Response
description: This module equips staff with the knowledge to identify, classify, respond to, and learn from information security incidents in accordance with ISO 27001 requirements.

sections.title: What Constitutes a Security Incident
sections.content:
An information security incident is any event that compromises or threatens the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of information or information systems. Understanding what constitutes an incident is essential for timely detection and response.

Incidents can range from confirmed breaches — such as unauthorised access to a database, ransomware encryption of files, or theft of a laptop containing sensitive data — to events that indicate a potential compromise, such as a phishing email received by multiple employees, unexpected system behaviour, or discovery of a previously unknown vulnerability being actively exploited.

It is important to distinguish between security events and security incidents. A security event is any observable occurrence in a system or network, such as a user logging in or a firewall blocking a connection. A security incident is one or more related security events that have a significant probability of compromising business operations or threatening information security. Not every event is an incident, but every incident begins as an event.

Organisations should maintain a clear definition of what constitutes a reportable incident, supported by examples relevant to their environment. This definition should be communicated to all staff to ensure that potential incidents are recognised and reported without delay. Err on the side of reporting — it is far better to investigate a non-event than to miss a genuine incident.

sections.title: Incident Classification and Triage
sections.content:
Effective incident response depends on rapid and accurate classification. Classification determines the severity, priority, and escalation path for each incident, ensuring that resources are directed where they are most needed.

A common classification scheme uses severity levels based on the potential or actual impact. For example: critical (widespread compromise, significant data breach, business operations halted), high (targeted attack, limited data exposure, significant system impairment), medium (contained incident, potential for escalation, localised disruption), and low (minor policy violation, unsuccessful attack attempt, minimal impact).

Triage is the initial assessment performed when an incident is reported. The goal is to confirm that a genuine incident has occurred, gather essential information, and assign an appropriate classification. Key questions during triage include: what systems and data are affected, is the incident ongoing, how many users or customers are impacted, is there evidence of data exfiltration, and what is the potential regulatory or contractual impact.

Classification may change as more information becomes available. An incident initially assessed as low severity may be upgraded if investigation reveals a broader compromise. Escalation procedures should be clearly defined for each severity level, including who must be notified, within what timeframe, and through which communication channels. Regulatory notification requirements — such as those under the UK GDPR — may impose strict deadlines for reporting certain types of incidents to supervisory authorities.

sections.title: Response Procedures
sections.content:
A structured response procedure ensures that incidents are handled consistently, efficiently, and effectively. The response should follow predefined steps while allowing flexibility for the specific circumstances of each incident.

The immediate priority is containment — limiting the scope and impact of the incident. Short-term containment actions might include isolating affected systems from the network, disabling compromised accounts, blocking malicious IP addresses, or revoking access tokens. These actions should be taken quickly but carefully, with consideration for evidence preservation and the impact on business operations.

Once the incident is contained, the focus shifts to eradication — removing the root cause and any artefacts left by the attacker. This may involve removing malware, patching vulnerabilities, resetting credentials, rebuilding compromised systems, or correcting misconfigurations. Eradication must be thorough; leaving any foothold allows the attacker to return.

Recovery involves restoring affected systems and services to normal operation. This includes restoring data from clean backups, bringing systems back online in a controlled manner, verifying that systems are functioning correctly, and monitoring closely for signs of recurring activity. Recovery should be prioritised based on business criticality, and systems should not be returned to production until there is confidence that the threat has been fully eliminated.

sections.title: Evidence Preservation
sections.content:
Preserving evidence is essential for understanding the full scope of an incident, supporting legal or disciplinary proceedings, meeting regulatory obligations, and improving future defences. Improper handling of evidence can render it inadmissible or unreliable.

Digital evidence is volatile and easily altered. The order in which evidence is collected matters — prioritise the most volatile data first. This typically means capturing the contents of system memory (RAM) before disk images, as memory contents are lost when a system is powered off or rebooted. Network traffic captures, log files, and system configurations should also be collected promptly.

Maintain a strict chain of custody for all evidence. Document who collected each item, when it was collected, how it was stored, and who has accessed it since collection. Use write-blockers when imaging hard drives, calculate and record cryptographic hashes to verify integrity, and store evidence in tamper-evident containers. Digital evidence should be stored on isolated, secure systems with restricted access.

Avoid actions that could contaminate evidence. Do not log into compromised systems using the attacker's credentials, do not run unnecessary commands on affected systems, and do not attempt to clean up before evidence has been collected. If you are uncertain about the correct procedure, consult your incident response team or a digital forensics specialist before taking any action.

sections.title: Lessons Learned and Continuous Improvement
sections.content:
Every security incident, regardless of severity, presents an opportunity to strengthen the organisation's defences. A formal lessons-learned process ensures that the knowledge gained from each incident is captured and acted upon.

Conduct a post-incident review as soon as practicable after the incident has been resolved. This review should involve all key participants — the incident response team, affected business units, IT operations, and management. The review should be constructive, focusing on process improvement rather than blame.

Key questions to address include: how was the incident detected and could detection have been faster, were response procedures followed and were they adequate, what worked well and what could be improved, what was the root cause and has it been fully addressed, and are there systemic issues that contributed to the incident. Document the findings, agree on specific remedial actions, assign ownership, and set deadlines for completion.

Feed the outcomes back into the ISMS. Update risk assessments to reflect the new threat intelligence, revise incident response procedures to address any gaps, enhance monitoring and detection capabilities, and adjust training programmes to address the human factors involved. Track remedial actions through to completion and verify their effectiveness. This cycle of continuous improvement is a core requirement of ISO 27001 and is essential to maintaining an effective security posture.

title: Data Classification & Handling
description: This module explains how to classify information assets according to their sensitivity and business value, and how to handle, label, and transfer information securely at each classification level.

sections.title: Classification Levels
sections.content:
Data classification is the process of categorising information based on its sensitivity, value, and the impact that would result from its unauthorised disclosure, modification, or loss. A clear classification scheme enables consistent protection of information across the organisation.

A typical classification scheme includes three or four levels. Public information is intended for general release and carries no confidentiality requirements. Internal information is intended for use within the organisation and should not be disclosed externally without authorisation. Confidential information is sensitive and its disclosure could cause significant harm to the organisation, its customers, or its partners. Restricted information is the most sensitive category, where unauthorised disclosure could result in severe legal, financial, or reputational consequences.

Each classification level should be clearly defined, with practical examples relevant to your organisation. For instance, marketing brochures may be public, internal policies may be internal, customer personal data may be confidential, and merger and acquisition plans may be restricted. The classification should reflect the highest sensitivity of any information contained within a document or dataset.

Classification is the responsibility of the information owner — the person or role accountable for the asset. Information should be classified at the point of creation or receipt and reviewed periodically, as sensitivity may change over time. Over-classification is wasteful and leads to control fatigue, while under-classification exposes the organisation to unnecessary risk.

sections.title: Labelling Requirements
sections.content:
Labelling makes the classification of information visible to anyone who handles it, enabling them to apply the appropriate level of protection. Without clear labelling, people cannot be expected to treat information correctly.

All documents, files, emails, and other information assets should be labelled with their classification level. For electronic documents, this typically means including the classification in the header or footer, in the file name or metadata, and in the subject line of emails. For physical documents, the classification should be clearly marked on the cover page, each subsequent page, and on any storage containers such as folders or envelopes.

Labelling should be applied consistently using the organisation's standard terms and formatting. Automated tools can assist with labelling by prompting users to classify information at the point of creation, applying visual markings, and embedding metadata for use by data loss prevention (DLP) systems. These tools reduce the burden on individuals and improve consistency.

Where information from different classification levels is combined, the resulting asset should be labelled at the highest applicable level. When classification changes — for example, when confidential information is approved for public release — labels must be updated accordingly. Mislabelled information is a common finding in audits and a practical risk that can lead to inadequate protection or unnecessary restrictions.

sections.title: Handling Procedures for Each Level
sections.content:
Each classification level requires specific handling procedures that govern how information may be stored, accessed, copied, transmitted, and disposed of. These procedures must be practical, clearly documented, and communicated to all relevant personnel.

Public information has minimal handling restrictions but should still be accurate and authorised for release. Internal information should be stored on access-controlled systems, shared only within the organisation, and disposed of securely when no longer needed. Standard organisational security controls — such as network access controls, encrypted laptops, and secure disposal — are generally sufficient.

Confidential information requires stronger controls. It should be encrypted at rest and in transit, stored only on approved and access-controlled systems, and shared only with authorised individuals on a need-to-know basis. Printing should be minimised, and physical copies must be stored in locked cabinets or secure areas. Disposal must use cross-cut shredding for paper and verified data wiping or destruction for electronic media.

Restricted information demands the highest level of protection. Access should be strictly limited and individually authorised by the information owner. Encryption must use strong, current algorithms. Distribution should be tracked and logged. Storage locations must have enhanced physical and logical security. Any reproduction or transfer requires explicit authorisation. When restricted information is no longer needed, destruction must be witnessed and recorded.

sections.title: Information Transfer Security
sections.content:
Transferring information — whether electronically, physically, or verbally — introduces risks that must be managed according to the classification of the information involved. Secure transfer procedures protect information while it is in transit and ensure it reaches only the intended recipient.

Electronic transfers should use encrypted channels appropriate to the classification level. For confidential and restricted information, this means end-to-end encryption using approved tools and protocols. Standard email is generally not suitable for sensitive information unless encryption (such as S/MIME or PGP) is applied. Secure file transfer services, encrypted messaging platforms, and virtual private networks (VPNs) provide more appropriate options.

Physical transfers of sensitive documents or media should use tracked courier services with tamper-evident packaging. The recipient should confirm receipt, and the tracking record provides an audit trail. Removable media — such as USB drives — should be encrypted and used only when no suitable electronic transfer method is available. The use of personal removable media should be prohibited.

Verbal disclosure of sensitive information also requires care. Be aware of your surroundings when discussing confidential matters — avoid conversations in public areas, shared workspaces, or on public transport where you may be overheard. When using teleconference or video conferencing tools, verify participant identities and ensure the platform meets your organisation's security requirements. Information shared verbally is just as sensitive as information in writing.

title: Supplier & Third-Party Security
description: This module addresses the information security risks associated with suppliers and third parties, covering risk assessment, contractual requirements, ongoing monitoring, and the specific considerations for cloud services.

sections.title: Supplier Risk Assessment
sections.content:
Organisations increasingly rely on suppliers and third parties to deliver services, process data, and support business operations. Each supplier relationship introduces information security risks that must be identified, assessed, and managed. A compromise at a supplier can directly impact your organisation's security.

Supplier risk assessment should be conducted before entering into a new relationship and reviewed periodically throughout the engagement. The assessment should consider the type and sensitivity of information the supplier will access or process, the criticality of the service to business operations, the supplier's security maturity and track record, and the regulatory and legal environment in which they operate.

Use a structured approach to categorise suppliers by risk level. High-risk suppliers — those with access to confidential or restricted information, or those providing critical services — require more rigorous assessment, which may include security questionnaires, review of certifications (such as ISO 27001 or SOC 2), on-site audits, and penetration test results. Lower-risk suppliers may be assessed through lighter-touch methods such as self-assessment questionnaires and review of publicly available information.

Maintain a register of all suppliers with access to organisational information, including the nature of the relationship, the information shared, the risk rating, and the date of the last assessment. This register is essential for managing supplier risks holistically and for demonstrating compliance during audits.

sections.title: Security Requirements in Agreements
sections.content:
Contractual agreements with suppliers must clearly define information security requirements, responsibilities, and obligations. Verbal assurances and assumed standards are insufficient — security expectations must be explicitly documented and agreed upon.

Key security clauses to include in supplier agreements cover: the scope of information access and processing, required security controls and standards, data protection and privacy obligations, incident notification requirements and timeframes, right to audit and assess compliance, sub-contractor management and approval processes, data return and destruction on termination, and liability and indemnification for security breaches.

Requirements should be proportionate to the risk level of the supplier relationship. A supplier processing restricted personal data requires significantly more detailed contractual obligations than a supplier providing stationery. Where the supplier is processing personal data on your behalf, a data processing agreement compliant with the UK GDPR is a legal requirement.

Agreements should also address how changes are managed — including changes to services, personnel, sub-contractors, and security controls — and how disputes regarding security requirements are resolved. Non-disclosure agreements should be in place before any confidential information is shared. Review and update supplier agreements periodically to reflect changes in services, risks, and regulatory requirements.

sections.title: Monitoring Supplier Compliance
sections.content:
Establishing security requirements in contracts is necessary but not sufficient. Ongoing monitoring is essential to verify that suppliers are meeting their obligations and that the security of your information is being maintained throughout the relationship.

Monitoring activities should be proportionate to the risk level of the supplier. For high-risk suppliers, this may include regular review of security reports and certifications, periodic audits (conducted by your organisation or an independent third party), review of incident and breach reports, service level monitoring, and vulnerability assessment results. For lower-risk suppliers, periodic self-assessment questionnaires and review of updated certifications may be adequate.

Establish clear governance structures for supplier security management. Define who is responsible for managing each supplier relationship, how frequently reviews are conducted, what information the supplier must provide, and how non-compliance is escalated and addressed. Regular meetings with key suppliers should include security as a standing agenda item.

When monitoring reveals that a supplier is not meeting its security obligations, take prompt action. This may range from agreeing a remediation plan with defined timelines through to suspending or terminating the relationship if the risk is unacceptable. Document all monitoring activities, findings, and actions taken. This evidence demonstrates due diligence and supports continuous improvement of your supplier security programme.

sections.title: Cloud Service Security
sections.content:
Cloud services present specific information security considerations that go beyond traditional supplier management. The shared responsibility model, the dynamic nature of cloud environments, and the concentration of data in cloud platforms require particular attention.

Understand the shared responsibility model for each cloud service. In infrastructure-as-a-service (IaaS), the cloud provider secures the underlying infrastructure while you are responsible for the operating system, applications, and data. In platform-as-a-service (PaaS), the provider takes on more responsibility but you still manage applications and data. In software-as-a-service (SaaS), the provider manages almost everything but you remain responsible for access management, data classification, and user behaviour.

Key considerations for cloud security include data residency and sovereignty (where your data is physically stored and processed, and which legal jurisdictions apply), encryption of data at rest and in transit, identity and access management integration, logging and monitoring capabilities, backup and disaster recovery arrangements, and the provider's incident response and notification procedures.

Before adopting a cloud service, conduct a thorough risk assessment that considers the sensitivity of the data involved, the criticality of the service, and the provider's security posture. Review the provider's certifications, audit reports, and security documentation. Ensure that your organisation retains the ability to extract its data in a usable format if the relationship ends. Cloud lock-in — where migration away from a provider becomes prohibitively difficult — is a strategic risk that should be considered alongside information security risks.
